agent_output: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"HTTP ingestion endpoint","to":"internal event pipeline","status":"correct","issue":"Well-instrumented with request IDs and structured logging"},{"from":"event pipeline","to":"data warehouse","status":"broken","issue":"Non-idempotent writes combined with at-least-once delivery guarantee means duplicate records are created under normal operation — not just edge cases. Every ACK-loss retry, every circuit breaker recovery, every pod restart produces silent data corruption in the warehouse."},{"from":"event pipeline","to":"dead-letter queue","status":"broken","issue":"DLQ exists but has no consumer, no alerting on depth, and no reprocessing path. Failed events silently accumulate and are effectively lost — the DLQ provides a false sense of safety."},{"from":"retry mechanism","to":"data warehouse","status":"broken","issue":"Exponential backoff retries re-submit events without idempotency keys. The retry mechanism actively causes the data integrity problem it was designed to mitigate."}],"contract_compliance":"The service promises event delivery but cannot guarantee exactly-once semantics. Downstream consumers and analysts will see inflated counts, corrupted funnels, and unreliable metrics. For an analytics ingestion service, data accuracy IS the contract — and it is violated.","data_flow_integrity":"Compromised. The happy path works, but any disruption — network blip, warehouse latency spike, pod restart, circuit breaker trip — injects duplicate records with no detection or correction mechanism. The at-least-once guarantee without deduplication means the error rate compounds over time and under load."},"observability_review":{"logging":{"coverage":"adequate","gaps":[]},"metrics":{"coverage":"Strong — Prometheus histograms, p99 dashboards, and alerts are configured","gaps":["No metric tracking duplicate event rate or deduplication failures","No metric on DLQ depth or age of oldest message in DLQ","No metric distinguishing genuine new writes from retry-induced duplicate writes"]},"tracing":{"coverage":"Good — OpenTelemetry distributed tracing in place","gaps":["Traces likely cannot distinguish a duplicated event from a unique event without an idempotency key to correlate on"]},"debugging_capability":"Good for latency and throughput issues. Poor for data integrity issues — an on-call engineer at 3am would have no way to determine how many duplicate records were injected during an incident, nor any way to remediate them after the fact."},"resilience_assessment":{"failure_modes":[{"scenario":"ACK lost after successful warehouse write — event retried","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"MUST-HAVE: Add an idempotency key (event_id + timestamp hash) to every event at ingestion time. Warehouse writes must use UPSERT or INSERT-IF-NOT-EXISTS keyed on this ID. This is the single most critical fix."},{"scenario":"Circuit breaker trips, queued events replayed on recovery","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Same idempotency key fix resolves this. Without it, every circuit breaker recovery event is a data corruption event."},{"scenario":"Dead-letter queue fills up indefinitely","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"MUST-HAVE: Deploy a DLQ consumer that either retries with backoff or routes to a human-reviewable error store. Add alerting on DLQ depth > threshold and oldest message age > threshold. Without this, the DLQ is a write-only graveyard."},{"scenario":"Sustained load spike beyond 10K events/sec","handling":"partial","recommendation":"NICE-TO-HAVE: Add backpressure signaling (HTTP 429 with Retry-After) so upstream callers can shed load gracefully rather than timing out and retrying, which compounds the duplication problem."},{"scenario":"Data warehouse goes down for extended period","handling":"partial","recommendation":"Circuit breaker prevents cascade, but events accumulate in the DLQ with no consumer. Extended outage means permanent data loss since the DLQ is never drained."}],"retry_config":"Exponential backoff with 3 retries is well-configured mechanically, but actively harmful without idempotent writes — each retry is a duplicate injection.","timeout_config":"Not specified, but acceptable given other resilience measures.","circuit_breakers":"Present and correctly placed on warehouse writes. However, recovery from open→closed state replays events without deduplication, causing a burst of duplicates.","graceful_degradation":"Partial. The DLQ captures failures but never processes them, so degradation is actually silent data loss with extra steps."},"operational_readiness":{"deployment_ready":false,"rollback_capable":true,"rollback_plan":"Blue/green deployment with load balancer traffic switch enables instant rollback. This is well-designed.","monitoring_coverage":"Strong for availability and latency. Blind spot on data integrity — no way to detect or measure duplicate records entering the warehouse.","on_call_documentation":"Needs runbook entries for: (1) how to identify and purge duplicate records after an incident, (2) how to drain and reprocess the DLQ, (3) what duplicate rate is acceptable vs actionable.","new_infrastructure_required":{"new_env_vars":[],"new_services":["DLQ consumer/reprocessor service"],"new_queues_topics":[],"config_changes":["Warehouse write path must change to UPSERT or conditional INSERT with idempotency key"],"infra_provisioning":["Alerting rules for DLQ depth and oldest message age"]},"database_safety":{"has_migrations":true,"reversible":false,"data_preserving":false,"large_table_impact":true,"details":"Non-idempotent writes to a data warehouse at 10K events/sec will accumulate duplicate rows that are expensive to identify and delete retroactively. Adding a unique constraint or dedup key after the fact requires scanning the entire table and may cause extended locking on large tables."},"verdict":"NOT READY"},"escalations":[{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"Non-idempotent warehouse writes with at-least-once delivery","reason":"This is not an edge case — it is a guaranteed data corruption path under normal operation. Every network hiccup, every retry, every circuit breaker recovery silently injects duplicate records into the warehouse. For an analytics service, corrupted data is worse than missing data because it erodes trust in all downstream metrics and decisions. This must be fixed before production traffic touches the service.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"infrastructure_gap","issue":"Dead-letter queue has no consumer or alerting","reason":"The DLQ creates a false safety net. Failed events go in but never come out. No alerts fire when it grows. During an extended warehouse outage, this means silent permanent data loss. A DLQ consumer and depth-based alerting are required before shipping.","routed_to":"Coach K"}],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["The idempotency gap is a definitive blocking issue — not a judgment call","DLQ without consumer is a clear operational gap","Observability and deployment story are genuinely strong"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact warehouse technology and whether it natively supports UPSERT semantics","Whether upstream clients have natural idempotency keys that could be reused"],"assumptions":["The data warehouse supports some form of conditional/idempotent write operation","The DLQ is a standard message queue (SQS, Kafka DLT, etc.) that supports consumer attachment","Load test results at 10K/sec are representative of production traffic patterns"]}}
